text:
This paper offers a hermeneutical study of the resurrection of our Lord Jesus Christ as described in Q&A 45 of the Heidelberg Catechism. Exploring the historical and theological background of the text using reference materials including the work of Zacharias Ursinus, Reformation writings, and the New Testament and early church background, the authors seek to understand and describe the intended meaning of the Catechism authors.

As a case study for biblical exegesis this article focuses on the hermeneutical tension existing in the Easter narratives, dealing with both the subjective and the objective aspects of Scripture interpretation. The authors deplore the almost universal one-sided existential interpretation of the Easter message in contemporary scholarship. To this often existentially Kantian, sometimes Gnostic, spirit- or soul-orientated way of interpretation, which turns the attention away from the historically given in favour of the existence of man, the authors provide a counterbalance within the scope of this article. They take the Heidelberg Catechism and with it the Apostle’s confession of faith as their starting point, and show in what sense the so-called ‘physicality’ of the Easter narratives are necessary for the justification of the theological terms used in the Catechism to describe man’s redemption in Christ, the new creation of man in Him and the believer’s hope of future glory.

Using Diltheys terms and concepts of “fusion of horizons,” it explores how the Catechism relates to a monistic anthropology or doctrine of human being in an embaced Christology, rejecting a Platonist dualism. Special attention is given to the work of Arniske’s commentary on Ursinus, and the ecumenical affirmations of the Early Fathers of the Church. The new life promised in the Catechism, a doctrine of remarkable inclusivity and promise, is necessarily connected to the resurrection of Jesus Christ, in this being the coming reality of humanity’s salvation.
